stories.seo_title: Why cats purr — it’s not just happiness 🐱
stories.yt_description: Cats purr. We all know that. But here’s why. Cats purr to heal their own bones. They’re basically doing DIY physiotherapy every day. Source: Pexels
stories.hook: Cats purr to heal their bones.
stories.script: Cats purr to heal their own bones. That rumble isn’t just for happiness—it’s at a frequency that speeds up healing. Kittens purr while nursing, but adults do it when injured too. Even big cats like cheetahs purr nonstop. How’s your cat’s DIY recovery routine going?

stories.seo_title: Why cats purr — it isn't just happiness 🐈
stories.yt_description: Cats. Turns out their purrs aren’t just for happiness. It’s also how they heal their own bones. Source: Pexels
stories.hook: Cats purr to heal their own bones.
stories.script: Cats purr to heal their own bones. A cat’s purr vibrates at 25–150 Hz — the same range that boosts bone density. That’s why injured cats purr so much. Even big cats like cheetahs do it. So next time your cat purrs, maybe they’re fixing themselves. What’s your cat’s weirdest habit?

stories.seo_title: Dogs remember human faces for years 🐶
stories.yt_description: Dogs remember human faces for years. Studies show they can pick out a familiar face even after long gaps. Source: Pexels

stories.seo_title: Why border collies can’t stop running 🐕
stories.yt_description: Everything you think you know about border collies is wrong. Their brains are wired to run—literally. Source: Pexels
stories.hook: Their brains are wired to run.
stories.script: Their brains are wired to run. A border collie’s mind is built for constant motion—herding sheep used to keep them moving for 12 hours straight. They don’t just like to run; their bodies release feel-good chemicals when they do. Ever seen one ‘chase’ shadows? It’s not boredom. It’s their brain demanding more action. What’s the longest your dog has run just to feel right?

stories.seo_title: Why whales have earwax that tells their age 🐋
stories.yt_description: Whales. Their earwax builds up in layers like tree rings. One plug can reveal the animal’s entire life story. Source: Pexels
stories.hook: A whale’s earwax reveals its age.
stories.script: A whale’s earwax reveals its age. Each dark plug layers like tree rings—one plug can tell the whole life story. Teen stress shows in yellow bands. Pregnancy leaves a distinct stripe. It’s like reading a whale diary underwater. How old do you think the biggest plug ever found was?

stories.title: aerial footage of whales in the ocean
stories.description: A clip of marine life in the ocean: aerial footage of whales in the ocean
stories.seo_title: Whales can talk in dialects that change like pop songs
stories.yt_description: A clip of whales in the ocean. Whales don't just sing random tunes—they have regional dialects that evolve over time, like pop songs changing with the times. Source: Pexels
stories.hook: Whales rewrite their songs every decade.
stories.script: Whales rewrite their songs every decade. Humpbacks off Australia sing totally different tunes than ones near Hawaii. Their ‘dialects’ even drift like pop charts—new clicks and chirps enter every few years. Scientists recorded one pod’s track change in just 27 months. So next time you hear whale song, imagine it’s a whale remix hitting the dance floor. Have you heard whale songs before?

stories.seo_title: Bears: why baby bears have no dads
stories.yt_description: Bears. Female bears can give birth without a male partner. The father's DNA isn't even present in the cubs. Source: Pexels

stories.script: Female bears can ditch dads entirely. Some give birth without a male, their eggs just clone themselves. The cubs grow up with no father’s DNA at all. Watch this one twice—it gets weirder.

stories.seo_title: Bears hug trees to stay cool and calm 🐻
stories.yt_description: Black bears hug trees to hug the chill right out of their day. They also leave scent tags like living graffiti. Source: Pexels
stories.hook: Bears hug trees to stay cool.
stories.script: Bears hug trees to stay cool. Their fur traps heat, so pressing against bark drops their temperature by 20°F in minutes. stories.seo_title: Why sloth bears flip rocks and sniff the air 🐻
stories.yt_description: Meet the sloth bear—this shaggy giant doesn’t just dig for ants, it flips rocks to find them. The most surprising part? It covers its nose with its paw to keep ants from crawling up while it eats. Source: Pexels
stories.hook: Sloth bears flip rocks for snacks.
stories.script: Sloth bears flip rocks for snacks. But here’s the part nobody tells you: they cover their noses with their paws while eating. Why? To stop ants from marching up their snouts. Oh, and their cubs ride on their backs—literally. Ever seen a bear with a backpack? Thought so. What’s your favorite bear behavior?

stories.yt_description: Did you know bears roll in flowers? They’re not just playing—their fur soaks up the scent, which masks their own smell. Source: Pexels
stories.hook: Bears roll in flowers for a smell you won’t guess.

stories.seo_title: Why wild horses run in circles at dawn 🐎
stories.yt_description: Wild horses at dawn. It’s not just about speed or energy—here’s the real reason they circle. Source: Pexels
stories.hook: Wild horses circle to survive.
stories.script: Wild horses circle to survive. It’s not just about energy—it flattens grass for safer sleep and scares off predators with a wall of hooves. Their eyes are on the sides of their heads, so they can spot danger while they rest. Watch the herd pull this off at sunrise every single day. Ever wondered why they rotate leaders? Think about it in the comments.

stories.seo_title: Why white horses aren’t actually white 🐴
stories.yt_description: White horse. They're not actually white—their skin is pink under every spot. Source: Pexels
stories.hook: This horse isn’t white.
stories.script: This horse isn’t white. Their fur looks bright, but their skin is pink under every spot. They’re born any color and lighten with age. Their real coat is a mix of grey and white hairs. Ever petted one and wondered why they’re so warm? Next time, check the skin. What color did you expect to see?
